title: Quiz: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later
description: Test your understanding of HNDL attacks and their implications

questions.question: What is a 'Harvest Now, Decrypt Later' (HNDL) attack?

options.text: Stealing decryption keys from a database
Collecting encrypted data today to decrypt with future quantum computers
Breaking encryption in real-time using classical computers

questions.explanation: HNDL attacks involve adversaries intercepting and storing encrypted communications today, with the plan to decrypt them later when quantum computers become available.

questions.question: If CRQC is expected in 2040 and your data must remain confidential for 30 years, what is your effective protection deadline?
questions.explanation: Effective deadline = CRQC date - sensitivity lifetime = 2040 - 30 = 2010. This means data transmitted after 2010 with 30-year sensitivity is already at risk!

questions.question: Which type of data is MOST at risk from HNDL attacks?

options.text: Session tokens that expire in 24 hours

questions.explanation: Data with very long sensitivity periods (like classified documents) is most at risk because it will still be valuable when quantum computers arrive. Short-lived data like session tokens is less vulnerable.

questions.question: Historical encrypted data that was transmitted years ago can be retroactively protected against HNDL attacks.
questions.explanation: False. Data already transmitted and potentially captured cannot be protected retroactively. If adversaries have stored encrypted traffic from past years, that data will be decryptable when CRQC arrives.

questions.question: Who are the most likely actors conducting HNDL operations?
questions.explanation: Nation-state intelligence agencies have the resources (massive storage capacity), motivation (long-term intelligence advantage), and patience (decades) for HNDL operations.

questions.question: What is the recommended immediate protection strategy against HNDL?

options.text: Wait for quantum computers to be built

questions.explanation: Hybrid encryption combining classical algorithms (for proven security) with post-quantum algorithms (for quantum resistance) provides defense in depth and is the recommended approach.

questions.question: Medical records typically have what sensitivity lifetime?
questions.explanation: Medical records have lifetime sensitivity (50+ years) because they contain permanent information about a person's health, genetic conditions, and medical history that remains sensitive for life.

questions.question: Which of the following can still be protected against HNDL? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Stored data that can be re-encrypted

questions.explanation: Future communications (a) can be protected with PQC, and stored data (c) can be re-encrypted with quantum-resistant algorithms. However, already-transmitted data (b, d) that may have been captured cannot be retroactively protected.
